hero.paragraph: I move between the city and the track, watching for moments that arrive without warning and vanish just as fast. A corner taken at the limit, a stranger caught mid-step, the light that lasts half a second before the city takes it back. I don't set the scene or retouch beyond the grade. Just the frame I caught.

body.p1: Photography started for me at Montmeló, behind a fence, when I was 13. F1 cars were vanishing into Turn 3 faster than I could properly see them, and I remember wanting to keep hold of that feeling: the noise, the speed, the split second before everything was gone. Years later my uncle put a camera in my hands, and I became obsessed with one question: how do you make a still image feel like 300 km/h?
body.p2: The street came later, almost as the opposite lesson. At the track you wait, read the line, trust the long lens. In Barcelona I try to disappear — 28mm, zone-focused, shooting from the hip, accepting that most frames will fail. But underneath, it is the same instinct: arrive early, stay honest, and press the shutter when the moment has just enough tension to live on its own.
body.p3: I don't retouch. The grade is where I shape the photograph's mood — sometimes with deep black and white, sometimes with colour that leans more emotional than literal. I am not trying to recreate exactly what was there; I am trying to protect what made me stop and take the frame in the first place. If the photograph has no truth before the grade, the grade will not save it.
body.sportGlass: Sony FE 200-600mm f/5.6-6.3 G OSS · Sony FE 70-200mm f/4 G OSS
body.streetGlass: Sony FE 28-60 mm F4-5,6 · Sony FE 50mm F1.8
